name: Normative Ethics
definition: Normative ethics studies principles and standards that guide what we ought to do, decide, or become.
examples: Assessing whether an action is permissible or required.

name: Consequentialism
definition: Consequentialism holds that the moral status of actions depends only on the consequences they produce.

sep_citation.title: Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: the view that normative properties depend only on consequences

examples: Evaluating choices by overall outcomes.

name: Utilitarianism
definition: Utilitarianism is a consequentialist view that identifies the good with welfare or happiness and directs us to maximize it impartially.

sep_citation.title: Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: The paradigm case of consequentialism is utilitarianism

examples: Choosing the policy that maximizes overall well-being.

name: Act Consequentialism
definition: Act consequentialism evaluates each action by whether it maximizes the good among available alternatives.

sep_citation.title: Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: an act is morally right if and only if that act maximizes the good

examples: Choosing the single act that yields the best outcome.

name: Rule Consequentialism
definition: Rule consequentialism assesses morality by rules selected for their consequences and then applies those rules to individual acts.

sep_citation.title: Rule Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: rules are to be selected solely in terms of their consequences

examples: Following a rule against lying because such rules have the best consequences overall.

name: Act Utilitarianism
definition: Act utilitarianism is act consequentialism with welfare or happiness as the good to be maximized.

sep_citation.title: Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: an act is morally right if and only if that act maximizes the good

examples: Choosing the act that maximizes total happiness.

name: Rule Utilitarianism
definition: Rule utilitarianism holds that the rightness of acts is determined by rules whose acceptance would maximize overall welfare.

sep_citation.title: Rule Consequentialism
sep_citation.quote: rules are to be selected solely in terms of their consequences

examples: Defending truth-telling as a welfare-maximizing rule.

name: Deontology
definition: Deontology grounds moral requirements in duties and constraints that are not reducible to consequences.

sep_citation.title: Deontological Ethics
sep_citation.quote: deontology is one of those kinds of normative theories regarding which choices are morally required, forbidden, or permitted

examples: Wrong to kill an innocent person even for greater good.

name: Kantian Ethics
definition: Kantian ethics emphasizes universalizable maxims and the requirement to treat persons as ends in themselves.
examples: Rejecting coercion that treats persons merely as means.

name: Rights-Based Deontology
definition: Rights-based deontology treats individual rights as moral constraints that limit permissible actions.
examples: Rights not to be harmed or killed constrain policy.

name: Threshold Deontology
definition: Threshold deontology holds that deontological constraints can be overridden when consequences cross a critical threshold.
examples: Constraints can yield when catastrophic harm is at stake.

name: Contractualism
definition: Contractualism grounds moral principles in what no one could reasonably reject as a basis for agreement among persons.
examples: Justifying rules by their acceptability to each person.

name: Virtue Ethics
definition: Virtue ethics centers moral evaluation on character and the cultivation of virtues.

sep_citation.title: Virtue Ethics
sep_citation.quote: Virtue ethics is currently one of three major approaches in normative ethics

examples: Assessing whether an action expresses honesty or courage.

name: Metaethics
definition: Metaethics studies the status, meaning, and epistemic standing of moral claims.
examples: Whether moral statements can be true or false.

name: Moral Cognitivism
definition: Moral cognitivism holds that moral statements express beliefs and are truth-apt.

sep_citation.title: Moral Cognitivism vs. Non-Cognitivism
sep_citation.quote: moral statements do express beliefs and that they are apt for truth and falsity

examples: Treating moral claims as candidates for truth.

name: Moral Realism
definition: Moral realism holds that moral claims purport to report facts and that at least some moral claims are true.

sep_citation.title: Moral Realism
sep_citation.quote: moral claims do purport to report facts and are true if they get the facts right

examples: Holding that some moral statements correspond to objective facts.

name: Error Theory
definition: Error theory is a cognitivist position claiming that moral statements purport to report facts but none are true.

sep_citation.title: Moral Realism
sep_citation.quote: deny that any moral claims are actually true

examples: Treating all moral claims as systematically false.

name: Moral Anti-Realism
definition: Moral anti-realism rejects at least one central commitment of moral realism, such as truth-aptness or factuality.
examples: Denying that moral claims report objective facts.

name: Moral Constructivism
definition: Moral constructivism explains normative truths through procedures or standards of practical reasoning rather than stance-independent moral facts.
examples: Testing principles by what agents could endorse under rational constraints.

name: Non-Cognitivism
definition: Non-cognitivism holds that moral statements are not truth-apt and do not express beliefs.

sep_citation.title: Moral Cognitivism vs. Non-Cognitivism
sep_citation.quote: moral statements have no substantial truth conditions

examples: Treating moral utterances as expressions of attitude.

name: Moral Epistemology
definition: Moral epistemology investigates whether and how moral knowledge or justified moral belief is possible.

sep_citation.title: Moral Epistemology
sep_citation.quote: How is moral knowledge possible? This question is central in moral epistemology

examples: Assessing evidence for moral beliefs.

name: Ethical Intuitionism
definition: Ethical intuitionism holds that some basic moral propositions are self-evident and that moral properties are non-natural.

sep_citation.title: Ethical Intuitionism
sep_citation.quote: basic moral propositions are self-evident, and moral properties are non-natural properties

examples: Treating some moral truths as immediately apprehensible.

name: Moral Skepticism
definition: Moral skepticism denies or doubts our moral knowledge or justification.

sep_citation.title: Moral Skepticism
sep_citation.quote: deny or raise doubts about common beliefs in various roles of reason in morality

examples: Questioning whether any moral beliefs are justified.

name: Moral Disagreement
definition: Moral disagreement emphasizes the prevalence of persistent moral conflict and its epistemic implications.

sep_citation.title: Moral Disagreement
sep_citation.quote: There is little controversy about the existence of widespread disagreement over moral issues

examples: Disputes over abortion, capital punishment, or animal ethics.

name: Moral Methodology
definition: Moral methodology covers the tools and procedures used to test, revise, and justify moral judgments and principles.
examples: Reflective equilibrium and thought experiments.

name: Moral Reasoning
definition: Moral reasoning concerns the practical reasoning agents use to decide what they morally ought to do.

sep_citation.title: Moral Reasoning
sep_citation.quote: practical reasoning about what, morally, they ought to do

examples: Balancing reasons to help, harm, or keep a promise.

name: Moral Dilemmas
definition: Moral dilemmas involve conflicts between moral requirements that appear to demand incompatible actions.

sep_citation.title: Moral Dilemmas
sep_citation.quote: Moral dilemmas, at the very least, involve conflicts between moral requirements

examples: Cases where duty and consequence appear to clash.

name: Reflective Equilibrium
definition: Reflective equilibrium is a method of mutual adjustment between considered judgments, principles, and background theories.

sep_citation.title: Reflective Equilibrium
sep_citation.quote: the mutual adjustment of principles and judgments in the light of relevant argument and theory

examples: Revising judgments or principles to improve overall coherence.

name: Narrow Reflective Equilibrium
definition: Narrow reflective equilibrium aligns principles with initial judgments, allowing limited adjustments to smooth inconsistencies.

sep_citation.title: Reflective Equilibrium
sep_citation.quote: a narrow reflective equilibrium is reached where principles are matched to initial judgments

examples: Adjusting a principle to fit a set of firm intuitions.

name: Wide Reflective Equilibrium
definition: Wide reflective equilibrium adds broader theoretical and empirical considerations when aligning judgments and principles.

sep_citation.title: Reflective Equilibrium
sep_citation.quote: A wide reflective equilibrium is achieved where the deliberator has brought judgments and principles into agreement

examples: Revising a judgment after considering background theories.

name: Thought Experiments
definition: Thought experiments use imagination to test the implications of moral principles and judgments.

sep_citation.title: Thought Experiment
sep_citation.quote: Thought experiments are basically devices of the imagination

examples: Trolley cases, lifeboat scenarios, and hypothetical dilemmas.
